entry.title: Stepping Outside of Your Comfort Zone
entry.summary: I looked through an old presentation I created years ago. I immediately felt appreciation for my past experiences. In 2017, I was tasked by my IT director to research five new things that I did not know of and present my findings to my fellow IT team members.
entry.content:
I looked through an old presentation I created years ago. I immediately felt appreciation for my past experiences. In 2017, I was tasked by my IT director to research five new things that I did not know of and present my findings to my fellow IT team members.

Of course, I cringed at the thought of standing in front of a room and speaking about something I was not fully versed in. What I did not know was that this simple task prepared me for my future.

Many people have asked me “how can I get into IT?” Curiosity and the ability to take the initiative, research many topics, and find the appropriate information will take you very far. Curiosity has always felt like an advantage.

Naturally, I am a creative person who enjoys being given a blank canvas and asked to come up with anything of my choosing to present. Back in school, I can remember being overjoyed when I arrived in my creative writing class to free write on any topic of our choosing. I also enjoyed writing about specific topics that were required for the course as well.

The five things project I was given years ago allowed me to think outside of the box and present things that some of the senior IT leadership may have known or were not aware of.

The benefit for me was that I wanted to use this opportunity to not only prove to my teammates that I took the task seriously, but also that I could share some of my personality with my teammates.

My 5 Things Project When I sat down to draft my presentation I considered the following:

Who is my target audience? What information do I want to share? How do I want to present the information? What is my deadline for completing the work? Finally, how can I add a little of my personality to the presentation? I designed the presentation to start with a familiar sight and sound to catch my viewer's attention.

I then added a gif as a play on words for my surname and my fictitious production companies’ branding

I have a love for music and animation. My daughter and I have watched the movie “Sing” many times. I enjoyed the scene where Buster Moon set up the theater to run on “Squid power”.

I used the same song used in the film “Flashing Lights” and since it was an Information Technology presentation, I found a gif of a server room with flashing lights.

As I have seen with so many presentations, I presented to my teammates as if they were my clients. I introduced myself and let them know I would be happy to answer their questions after my presentation. (I didn't think they would ask questions).

I learned from my speech coursework in high school and University that I could calm my nerves with some humor baked into my presentations. So I leaned in on that by presenting a few humorous slides in my deck.

Then I began my presentation to share the following topics I had researched: How to use Google Chrome’s developer tools to edit the text on a website. How to use God Mode on a Windows computer. How to remotely shut down your computer. How to use Windows 10 recorder software to record your screen. How to use an iPhone as a level when doing hanging items. How to use Google Chrome’s developer tools to edit the text on a website.

How to use God Mode on a Windows computer.

How to remotely shut down your computer.

How to use Windows 10 recorder software to record your screen.

How to use an iPhone as a level when doing hanging items.

Rolling Credits!

To make a long story short, I enjoyed the process of creating the project. 😀

Back then I did not consider how some of the elements of my assignment such as defining my target audience, and what information could be useful in other roles. While I ramp up to transition my career into technical writing I am enjoying the journey and look forward to continued learning.

Initially, I thought that my teammates would not be interested in the content I shared. They were interested, particularly in how to play around with developer tools.

In my opinion, it helps to work in an environment where your leadership encourages you to step outside of your comfort zone. What initially made me cringe with nervousness, gave me the confidence I needed to present information to customers, clients, and leaders. I am grateful for this experience.

entry.title: Experiences Will Travel
entry.summary: When I think about my career there are instances where I can connect something I learned on the job that I used in a future role.
entry.content:
When I think about my career there are instances where I can connect something I learned on the job that I used in a future role.

In my experience as a junior systems administrator, I was able to learn from a fantastic team of people. They are family. Some of my team was based in North Carolina and one of our IT Network Administrators and I were based in Texas. Our IT Director trusted me with many responsibilities. He would take the time to ask me questions that I had never been asked before. On one of his visits to the Texas site, he asked, “Where do you see your career in 5 years.” Our director changed and reshaped my way of thinking about my career and the path I wanted to take.

Our Database Administrators and IT Network Administrators were also fantastic. Each of them allowed me room to grow and make mistakes. Our IT Network Administrators shared their knowledge with me often. I learned how to carry myself as a professional, coding, systems administration, how to conduct a presentation, and Anime. They were all very approachable and assured me that if I had any questions to feel free to ask anytime.

On one occasion our IT Network Administrator in Texas took a scheduled trip to Orlando for Microsoft Ignite. While he was away, I was the only IT member on-site. One evening just about time for the end of my shift we received a notification that our IDF unit had lost network connectivity. I enjoy troubleshooting however this unit was about twenty-seven feet in the air inside of a distribution center. I could not resolve the issue without communicating and collaborating with my team so I leaned on our IT Network Administrator based in North Carolina.

I quickly reached out to our maintenance team who stopped what they were working on to share a harness with me. I safely rode the boom lift up as maintenance lifted me to the IDF unit. With our Network Administrator’s guidance, I rack-mounted a temporary switch and waited for the go-ahead before I removed the downed switch. With the ok, I removed the downed switch and took it to the IT office. I cleaned out the debris that had built up over time and caused the fans to no longer spin. This was the root cause for why the device was overheating. I tested the fans and then contacted maintenance again and went back up to mount the original switch. After configurations were done by the Network Administrator and confirmed I remounted the original switch then removed the temporary switch, and buttoned up the unit.

Was I afraid of the thought of going twenty-seven feet in the air? Yes. It was more important to me that I make my team proud and when it mattered most they could depend on me. It was my goal to help our organization get up and running as soon as possible. I learned a great deal that evening, that trusting in your teammates can do wonders for your team as well as empower you to think critically and achieve a common goal. I learned that it is okay to ask for help. I was also proud of myself for facing my fear of heights.

Our team eventually went through some restructuring, but we still keep in contact regularly. They are truly family. The organization and my team created an environment where I could take ownership, learn, and have pride in my work.

Jumping ahead in time, a few years later. I was in a completely different role with another organization. A similar scenario happened. This time, I knew what to do and handled the situation with much more confidence. I had no fear when going up to another IDF to handle an issue with a switch.

I am a believer that many of the experiences we gain are like tools in our tool bag. If an opportunity presents itself, You may want to say yes or offer some of your time to help out and what you learn could build lasting relationships and experience.

All the best,

Shane W.

entry.title: Create a Learning Environment with Microsoft 365
entry.summary: When learning new a concept it helps to have a method that best suits your learning style.
entry.content:
When learning new a concept it helps to have a method that best suits your learning style.

Some considerations:

Do you learn by reading the documentation?

Are you more of a visual learner?

Does getting your hands on the technology work best for you?

Here's what worked best for me:

Reading the material

Watching tutorials

Taking notes in my own words

Then I would create a test environment to see the expected behavior of the technology.

For example, if you wish to get more hands-on learning for Microsoft's tech stack, you may want to visit here

This can give you a better understanding when administering and troubleshooting applications such as SharePoint, Exchange Online, Microsoft Teams, and more.

If you would like to configure a test lab using the power of Microsoft 365, visit here Note: You can also compare home versions and business versions of Microsoft 365 here

Choose the option that best suits your need.

Choose "Try 1 month free"

Note: After your 1-month free trial, Microsoft 365 has a fee that will be charged. Your credit card is required. You can cancel anytime to stop future charges.

Depending on which option you choose, you will want to make note of when you start your free trial.

Enter the email address you wish to use to configure your tenant.

Then choose "Set up account"

Microsoft will want you to share your standard contact information such as name, company name, company size, and region.

Once you complete these steps choose "Next"

In the following step you will choose which method you would like to choose to send a verification code.

Choose your country code and enter your phone number.

Choose "Send verification code."

Once you receive your code you will enter it into the space provided on the page, then choose "Verify".

Next, you will create your username. Microsoft will provide a domain name as a suggestion you can change it now or later. Choose the option that best meets your need.

Note: You will see your username@domainname.onmicrosoft.com

Next set and confirm your password, and choose "next." The following step could seem scary, but keep in mind that the first month is free.

Verify your product name matches what you want to use for your free trial. For example, Microsoft 365 Business Standard Trial. You will see pricing and have an option for the quantity you wish to use.

-Choose "Add payment method" and enter your payment information and choose "Save."

Note: The "Trial details" section will show you how many users you can have during your trial and the free trial will be automatically converted to a paid annual subscription after one month. If you want to avoid charges it shows you the date you will want to cancel your subscription in the Microsoft 365 Admin center.

Finally, when you are ready you will choose "Start trial" And Voila!

You did it you now have 1 month to dive in and test out what Microsoft 365 has to offer.

Here are some resources to learn more about Microsoft 365.

Learning resources:

Microsoft Learn https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/learn/

Try Microsoft 365 for 1 month https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/try

Compare Microsoft 365 Business Products https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/business/compare-all-microsoft-365-business-products

All the best,

Shane W.

entry.title: A Win for Technical Documentation
entry.summary: Many times asking for help can open doors for you.
entry.content:
Many times asking for help can open doors for you.

In a previous role, I worked with a team that had limited interaction with an organization's internal Warehouse Management Software (WMS).

The organization's main distribution center site would frequently experience an issue where the printer queue which allowed them to print shipping labels would jam. Team leads would halt production and contact support and wait for a resolution. This could happen during business hours and at times when support staff would be out of the office, this resulted in slowed production.

At the time, the distribution center site was a startup environment where we all learned together, and pivoted to multiple issues, multiple times when necessary. Since the system was internal there was no documentation I could search for at the time and no google search would provide a resolution.

I contacted the lead software engineers who primarily worked with the WMS to request their time and gain an understanding of how the software was intended to work. I tested the functionality as a user, I was then able to proceed in documenting the steps to clear the printer queue both in the graphical user interface (GUI) and by a series of commands in the Linux-based version of the system.

I created how-to documents for team leads and internal IT support to use when clearing the print queues. Team leads and IT support team members could now follow the steps and assist the production team in getting up and running in less time. This documented process enabled a reduction in the distribution center's downtime during instances where the printer queues would jam in the future.

I am thankful to the engineers who took the time to meet with me during their busy workday and share their years of experience with me on the intricacies of the software.

This experience empowered me to know that no matter what the situation, collaboration, documentation, and thinking through the steps of a process can be beneficial to a team and create value for an organization.

If the opportunity presents itself, try asking for help you may be amazed at the results.

All the best,

Shane W.

entry.title: Books I Am Currently Reading
entry.summary: 👋 Greetings Earthlings! I hope you are safe and well! During the pandemic, I have like many others found new ways to learn and reinvent and entertain myself. I have discovered that the proper reading material can enrich your life as well as the lives of those you may come in contact with. {/ truncate /}I would like to share what I am currently reading. This week I am diving into How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie. I am looking forward to diving into this text as I have heard that it has some good insight into why people behave the way they do in our everyday lives. This insight can be quite useful in our professional and personal lives and can most definitely enhance one's soft skills. I am excited to read and interpret the text and see what important information I can glean from it and share with others.
entry.content:
👋 Greetings Earthlings! I hope you are safe and well! During the pandemic, I have like many others found new ways to learn and reinvent and entertain myself. I have discovered that the proper reading material can enrich your life as well as the lives of those you may come in contact with. I would like to share what I am currently reading. This week I am diving into How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie. I am looking forward to diving into this text as I have heard that it has some good insight into why people behave the way they do in our everyday lives. This insight can be quite useful in our professional and personal lives and can most definitely enhance one's soft skills. I am excited to read and interpret the text and see what important information I can glean from it and share with others.

That is all for now but I look forward to sharing more with you soon!

entry.title: How I Got Interested in Tech
entry.content:
👋 Greetings Earthlings!

Welcome to my blog space. My name is Shane I am super excited you are here.

I am an IT professional and lifelong learner who is forever curious about different forms of technology. As a kid, I was known to remove the parts from one set of toys and add it to another to make them not only look cooler but perform better and to my liking. I would take what I had around the house and create my own drum set. On many occasions, I would listen to my favorite tune and attempt to play it by ear on the family Casio.

As time went on when my family got our first PC I quickly became curious to know just how this amazing piece of machinery worked. To be honest I wanted to also know how I could play more Ski-Free, Chip’s Challenge the latest shareware games....

...I can’t begin to share how many times I broke the family computer. Thankfully my parents would send it off for repair and I would wait for it to return like a kid waiting on the first day of school. I am grateful to each of my parents for never scolding me for my curiosity and tinkering but encouraging it. After breaking the PC several times just trying to figure out how to open it and see what was inside I began to browse around and work to understand how to navigate the GUI. I learned through trial and error how to navigate directories and how they were structured. I learned how to utilize MS-DOS to run my favorite games. I began to see this PC as a way to learn and as a source of entertainment. This was a life-changing experience.

Soon after we begin to get free trial CDs to allow me to connect to the internet! Mind Blown. 🤯 I used our PC to learn as much as I could and soon began to be our family’s go-to person for PC repair and questions.

My early experiences with technology led me to the point I am at today in my life’s journey.

Thank you for learning a little bit about my earlier motivations for getting into tech. Soon, I will be using this space as a source to share some of my knowledge and some things I am learning, and projects I have been keeping busy with. So grab a snack or your favorite beverage and let’s learn and grow together!

I look forward to sharing more with you soon!

Shane

entry.title: Welcome
entry.summary: Welcome to my blog space. My name is Shane I am super excited you are here. {/ truncate /} I am documenting my experiences as journey from the IT help desk to technical writing. Also, feel free to take a look around.
entry.content: Welcome to my blog space. My name is Shane I am super excited you are here. I am documenting my experiences as journey from the IT help desk to technical writing. Also, feel free to take a look around.

entry.title: Happy New Year
entry.summary: Happy New Year 2021! Many are recovering from what has been a year of ups and downs. In my opinion, it is important to remember the ones we have lost.{/ truncate /} If you are reading this you may or may not have been directly affected by what is transpiring in the world today. I understand that for some it may be difficult to navigate and move forward in the middle of difficult times.
entry.content:
Happy New Year 2021! Many are recovering from what has been a year of ups and downs. In my opinion, it is important to remember the ones we have lost. If you are reading this you may or may not have been directly affected by what is transpiring in the world today. I understand that for some it may be difficult to navigate and move forward in the middle of difficult times.

If you are looking to make some changes or begin the process of figuring things out whether in life or career or just want something to do in your spare time there is one thing I would like to share. It may be best to figure out what your personality type is to begin taking the first steps to self-discovery.

One tool I use for this is the 16 Personalities test. My IT Director shared the 16 personalities test with me in 2017. I think it is not only a great tool to learn about yourself but even better to share with your friends, family, and co-workers. This tool can help you better understand your strengths and weaknesses as well as what type of environment you have more potential to thrive in.

All the best,

Shane
